text: The surgical team initiates the procedure using a robotic platform, performing an extensive Kocher maneuver to expose the vena cava and subsequently the left renal vein.
The surgeon references the superior mesenteric artery at its origin using a loop, then proceeds to free the artery in various directions.

The surgeon dissects the right edge of the hepato-duodenal pedicle, revealing a section of the biliary duct. Following the sectioning of the pyloric artery, a lymphadenectomy is initiated around the common hepatic artery, proper hepatic artery, portal vein, and left gastric artery.

The surgeon creates a tunnel behind the neck of the pancreas, positioned in front of the superior mesenteric vein, and places a loop for a pancreatic hanging maneuver to aid in the future sectioning of the pancreas. The caudal dissection of the superior mesenteric vein, marked by a loop, provides access to the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric artery. The arrival of the splenic vein is observed, and the pancreas is sectioned while tutoring the Wirsung duct.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to continue the dissection around the superior mesenteric artery, which was previously exposed using the Coker maneuver.

A loop is placed behind the duodenopancreatic block and passed below the superior mesenteric vein, facilitating the hanging of the mesopancreas structure, which includes fat, nerves, and nodes, and is innervated by the superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon performs a caudal to cranial section, visibly excising the hyperpancreatic duodenal artery between clips. The excision of the mesopancreas is extended towards the base of the celiac axis and the diaphragmatic pillars.
The completed TMPE (total mesopancreas excision) is displayed, highlighting the anatomical structures such as the superior mesenteric vein and the right edge of the superior mesenteric artery.
